findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Our client is..." — the universal MBB opener; every published McKinsey/BCG/Bain case begins with client identification. [McKinsey Careers — Interviewing](https://www.mckinsey.com/careers/interviewing)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Let me tell you my understanding of the case context and key objectives." — the play-back / "map habit" frame candidates are taught to use, and what good interviewers reward immediately. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB Guide](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Take a moment to think about this and walk me through how you'd approach it." — McKinsey-style standard handoff after the prompt. [IGotAnOffer — McKinsey case examples](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-examples)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "How would you think about this problem?" — McKinsey open-ended handoff. [Hacking the Case Interview — McKinsey Style](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mckinsey-style-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "What would you want to know?" — invitation to clarifying questions, standard McKinsey opener. [IGotAnOffer — Case examples](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-examples)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Why don't we start with you telling me what you'd want to investigate?" — Bain/BCG conversational variant. [Management Consulted — Case Interview Recap](https://managementconsulted.com/case-interview-recap/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Feel free to take notes." — standard permission-granting opener. [PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-to-ask-questions-in-case-interview-5988)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Do you have any clarifying questions before we dive in?" — explicit invitation; common at Bain. [CaseCoach — How interviews differ](https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Take a minute to organize your thoughts." — standard think-time grant; 60-90 sec is the norm. [Management Consulted — Case Interview Recap](https://managementconsulted.com/case-interview-recap/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "I'd like to walk you through a case our team recently worked on." — Bain/BCG conversational opener; signals interviewer-led case. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Where would you start?" — pure hypothesis-prompt opener at McKinsey. [Career In Consulting — Hypothesis-driven approach](https://careerinconsulting.com/hypothesis/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "What's your initial hypothesis?" — McKinsey prompt to force hypothesis-driven reasoning early. [Stratechi — How McKinsey uses hypotheses](https://www.stratechi.com/hypotheses/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "When you're ready, walk me through your approach." — pace handoff; signals 60-90 sec budget. [Hacking the Case Interview — Timing](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-timing)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Let me know when you're ready." — pace handoff variant. [PrepLounge — How long to think](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-long-should-you-be-taking-to-think-in-case-interviews-22402)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "We have about 30 minutes today." — time anchoring at the open; common Bain/BCG. [MyConsultingCoach — 4 steps](https://www.myconsultingcoach.com/case-interview-crack-a-case-in-4-steps)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Today I'd like to walk you through a problem I've been working on." — first-person Bain framing — interviewer presents the case as their own engagement. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Here's the situation." — neutral opener, leads directly into prompt. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB Guide](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "I'm going to give you some background, then I'll ask you a question." — interviewer-led McKinsey signal that the case will be segmented. [Caseinterview.com — Interviewer-led](https://caseinterview.com/interviewer-led-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "I have a few questions for you today, and we'll work through them together." — interviewer-led format flag. [Hacking the Case Interview — Interviewer-led](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/interviewer-led-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "The client has come to us asking..." — classic Bain/BCG framing; positions candidate as the consultant. [Crafting Cases — 9 best examples](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Don't worry about getting the right answer — I'm interested in how you think." — explicit reframe given to anxious candidates; common at Bain. [Sensei AI — Bain vs BCG vs McKinsey](https://www.senseicopilot.com/blog/bain-vs-bcg-vs-mckinsey-interview-differences-explained)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "I'd like you to think out loud as you go." — standard verbalization instruction. [PrepLounge — Case interview guide](https://www.preplounge.com/en/blog/consulting/interview/case-interview)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "I'll be playing the client today." — Bain framing for interviewer-as-client roleplay. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Let me give you the background." — neutral pre-prompt scaffolding. [Yale OCS — Case Interview](https://ocs.yale.edu/channels/the-case-interview/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Pause whenever you need to." — explicit grant of think-time mid-case. [PrepLounge — How long to think](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-long-should-you-be-taking-to-think-in-case-interviews-22402)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Take as much time as you need." — most generous version; usually means 60-90s ceiling in practice. [Hacking the Case Interview — Timing](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-timing)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "What questions do you have for me?" — explicit clarification handoff. [Hacking the Case Interview — Clarifying questions](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/clarifying-questions-to-ask-in-a-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Anything you'd like to clarify before we begin?" — Bain conversational variant. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "OK so to confirm…" — interviewer mirroring candidate's recap, used as a checkpoint. [Management Consulted — Recap](https://managementconsulted.com/case-interview-recap/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Ready when you are." — pace handoff signaling structure phase begins. [MyConsultingCoach](https://www.myconsultingcoach.com/case-interview-crack-a-case-in-4-steps)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "I'll repeat anything if you need." — explicit safety net offered on long prompts.

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Let me set the stage." — narrative-style opener, common in Bain. [Crafting Cases](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Tell me how you'd think about whether to enter the market." — typical M&A/market-entry prompt phrasing. [IGotAnOffer — Case examples](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-examples)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Walk me through your framework." — explicit structure handoff after think-time. [Casebasix — Structure flow](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/case-interview-structure-flow)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Why don't you start by telling me what you'd want to look at?" — soft handoff; Bain. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Let me know if anything in the prompt was unclear." — explicit invitation to clarify ambiguity. [Hacking the Case Interview — Clarifying questions](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/clarifying-questions-to-ask-in-a-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Imagine you're sitting with the CEO." — framing device to set urgency. [PrepLounge — 2026 guide](https://www.preplounge.com/en/blog/consulting/interview/case-interview)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "We've been engaged by [Client]." — Bain/McKinsey first-person plural framing. [Casebasix — MBB prep](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/mbb-case-interview-prep)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Their CEO has hired us to..." — explicit decision-maker anchor. [Crafting Cases](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "I'd like to focus on [specific question]." — narrowing prompt; typical interviewer-led move. [Caseinterview.com — Interviewer-led](https://caseinterview.com/interviewer-led-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Take a couple of minutes." — explicit 2-minute think budget. [PrepLounge — How long to think](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-long-should-you-be-taking-to-think-in-case-interviews-22402)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Take 30 seconds to think." — short variant; common when brainstorming question. [PrepLounge — Time allocation](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/case-interview-time-allocation-12400)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Let me give you a moment." — gentle pause-grant. [PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-long-should-you-be-taking-to-think-in-case-interviews-22402)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Just to be sure I'm explaining this clearly..." — interviewer self-check before re-stating prompt. [Management Consulted — Recap](https://managementconsulted.com/case-interview-recap/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Any questions on the prompt?" — terse clarification handoff. [PrepLounge — Clarifying questions](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/clarifying-questions-1786)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Let me read it once more." — accommodation when candidate looks lost.

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "I'll give you the same context I'd give a junior consultant on day one." — Bain framing emphasising realism. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Once you're ready, share your structure." — pace handoff explicitly tied to structure delivery. [Casebasix — Structure flow](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/case-interview-structure-flow)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "I want to see your thinking, not a memorised framework." — increasingly common explicit warning at Bain/BCG. [Leland — BCG guide](https://www.joinleland.com/library/a/breaking-down-bcg-interviews-what-to-expect-and-how-to-prepare)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Give me your initial reaction." — McKinsey hypothesis-prompt. [Stratechi](https://www.stratechi.com/hypotheses/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "What's your gut tell you?" — informal hypothesis-prompt. [Career In Consulting — Hypothesis](https://careerinconsulting.com/hypothesis/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Once you've structured the problem, walk me through it." — pace handoff for structure phase. [Casebasix — Structure flow](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/case-interview-structure-flow)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Where would you focus first?" — McKinsey prioritisation prompt. [Stratechi](https://www.stratechi.com/hypotheses/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "How would you go about answering this for the client?" — Bain conversational handoff. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/)

findings.section: 1.1 Standard openers (verbatim phrases real interviewers use)
findings.text: "Don't dive into numbers yet — I want your structure first." — common interviewer correction at the open. [Management Consulted — Top 10 mistakes](https://managementconsulted.com/top-10-case-interview-mistakes/)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Listen end-to-end without interrupting** the prompt before noting anything. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB Guide](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Pause briefly to organise thoughts** rather than starting to talk immediately.

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Restate the client's objective in their own words** as a one-sentence playback. [Management Consulted — Recap](https://managementconsulted.com/case-interview-recap/)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Flag the key constraint or context** explicitly (e.g. "5-year horizon, profitability lens").

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **State the success metric** ("So success = grow EBITDA by X%") before structuring. [PrepLounge — Clarifying](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/clarifying-questions-1786)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Ask 1-2 sharp clarifying questions, not more.** Ceiling is 2-3; more signals nervousness. [PrepLounge — How many clarifying questions](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-many-clarifying-questions-are-appropriate-and-when-should-they-be-asked-1869)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Confirm ambiguous terminology** ("Just to confirm — by 'profitability', do we mean EBITDA or net margin?"). [Hacking the Case Interview — Clarifying](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/clarifying-questions-to-ask-in-a-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Take notes in a structured layout** (objective top, facts left, framework right). [Casebasix — Structure flow](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/case-interview-structure-flow)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Echo numbers back** to confirm they heard correctly: e.g. "Great, so I think I heard all of the information correctly. There was one data point at the end that I would like to clarify. There's overall about 250,000 acres dedicated towards mango production. Is that correct?" — verbatim from a real Jenny Rae Le Roux mock. [Management Consulted — McKinsey Example](https://managementconsulted.com/mckinsey-case-interview-example-solved-by-consulting-candidate/)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Frame clarifications as "tell-then-verify"** rather than open queries: "It sounds like Agrochem is pretty diversified… I want to make sure that's appropriate in my set of assumptions." — preferred to "How big is Agrochem?" [Management Consulted — McKinsey Example](https://managementconsulted.com/mckinsey-case-interview-example-solved-by-consulting-candidate/)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Use 60-90 seconds for structure**, not more. [Hacking the Case Interview — Timing](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-timing)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Stay calm and quiet during think-time** — silence is fine if you're working. [Casebasix — Structure thinking](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/structure-thinking-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Open with a confident voice and posture.** Interviewers register tone before content. [Sensei AI — Bain vs BCG vs McKinsey](https://www.senseicopilot.com/blog/bain-vs-bcg-vs-mckinsey-interview-differences-explained)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Verbally signpost the playback.** "Let me make sure I have this right." [Management Consulted — Recap](https://managementconsulted.com/case-interview-recap/)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **State an early hypothesis** when invited. "My initial hunch is X, and here's why." [Career In Consulting — Hypothesis](https://careerinconsulting.com/hypothesis/)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Order clarifications by importance** — business model first, then objective scope, then constraints. [Hacking the Case Interview — Clarifying](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/clarifying-questions-to-ask-in-a-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Treat ambiguity as deliberate** — assume the interviewer left things vague to see if you'd surface them. [PrepLounge — Critique of structure](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/critique-of-this-case-interview-structure-904)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Recap *after* clarifying questions, not before** — clarify first, then confirm understanding. [Hacking the Case Interview — Clarifying](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/clarifying-questions-to-ask-in-a-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Say "thank you" or acknowledge** before pausing for think-time — small social cue interviewers notice. [PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-to-ask-questions-in-case-interview-5988)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Write down the question verbatim** so you can refer back. [Casebasix — Structure flow](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/case-interview-structure-flow)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Keep first questions short and crisp** — 1-2 sentences max each. [Hacking the Case Interview — Clarifying](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/clarifying-questions-to-ask-in-a-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Show ownership** — "Here's how I'd approach this for the client." [Leland — BCG guide](https://www.joinleland.com/library/a/breaking-down-bcg-interviews-what-to-expect-and-how-to-prepare)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Mirror the interviewer's framing language** — if they said "profitability", don't switch to "earnings". [Management Consulted — Recap](https://managementconsulted.com/case-interview-recap/)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Pause silently before talking** — 5-10 sec of composed silence reads as confidence. [Casebasix — Structure thinking](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/structure-thinking-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.2 What good candidates do in the first 30 seconds
findings.text: **Treat the recap as a handshake** — it builds rapport and signals listening. [Management Consulted — Recap](https://managementconsulted.com/case-interview-recap/)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Jumping into structure before confirming the objective.** Signals "showing off" not problem-solving. [Caseinterviewhub — 8 mistakes](https://www.caseinterviewhub.com/blog/mistakes-case-preparation-mckinsey-bcg-bain)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Asking 4+ clarifying questions** — reads as nervousness/stalling. [PrepLounge — How many clarifying questions](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-many-clarifying-questions-are-appropriate-and-when-should-they-be-asked-1869)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Weak opener like "I'm not sure what this means"** — signals uncertainty, breaks credibility instantly. [Casebasix — Clarifying questions](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/clarifying-questions-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **"So, um, can you tell me more?"** — same: low-confidence verbal pattern.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Asking analysis-style questions up front** ("How much are revenues declining?") — those belong in the body. [PrepLounge — Clarifying](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/clarifying-questions-1786)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Asking about competition/customer satisfaction up front** — should be inside framework, not pre-framework.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Talking through think-time aloud** the whole time — looks unable to think internally. [PrepLounge — How long to think](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-long-should-you-be-taking-to-think-in-case-interviews-22402)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Going silent for >3 minutes** — interviewers find it uncomfortable; talk through your logic if you must.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Forgetting to verbalise process** — "silence makes you look stuck." [Hacking the Case Interview — McKinsey Style](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mckinsey-style-case-interview)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Trying to "take over" a McKinsey case** as if it were Bain — looks uncoachable.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Pausing repeatedly to ask "how am I doing?"** — explicitly named a "bad thing to do." [RocketBlocks — Don't be that candidate](https://www.rocketblocks.me/blog/that-candidate.php)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Asking the interviewer "What about me would make a great consultant?"** — puts them on the spot. [RocketBlocks](https://www.rocketblocks.me/blog/that-candidate.php)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Trying to interrogate or trick the interviewer** — reverses the dynamic, reads as desperate.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Complaining about previous roles in fit chat** before the case — flags "complainer" persona. [RocketBlocks](https://www.rocketblocks.me/blog/that-candidate.php)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Misinterpreting the scope** because they didn't recap. [Caseinterviewhub — 8 mistakes](https://www.caseinterviewhub.com/blog/mistakes-case-preparation-mckinsey-bcg-bain)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Diving into a memorised framework verbatim** — interviewers spot it instantly and downgrade. [Casebasix — Frameworks](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/case-interview-frameworks-guide)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Force-fitting a profitability tree onto a market-entry case** — explicit named anti-pattern.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Rambling 30+ seconds before getting to the recap.** Communication = control; rambling = no control.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Skipping the recap entirely.** Common; major red flag because interviewer can't tell if you understood.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Hedging recap language** ("I think maybe the client wants…") — kill confidence.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Asking interviewer for permission to take notes** mid-prompt — shows uncertainty about basics.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Vague or hedging conclusions, mirrored at open** ("It depends…") — McKinsey explicitly penalises this.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Asking questions whose answers don't change your structure** — wastes the budget.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Burning >2 min on clarifications** — eats structure time.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Repeating the prompt word-for-word** instead of paraphrasing — adds zero signal.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Looking surprised by ambiguity** — interviewers expect ambiguity; surprise reads as inexperience.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Asking what framework to use** — biggest tell of an unprepared candidate.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Performing rehearsed cadence** ("step one… step two…") — flagged as "felt rehearsed and lacks creativity" feedback by McKinsey interviewers. [PrepLounge — Rehearsed feedback](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/i-have-recently-received-my-feedback-on-a-case-interview-the-interviewer-mentioned-that-my-performance-in-the-case-was-great-however-it-felt-rehearsed-and-lacks-creativity-what-does-it-mean-that-it-fe-21841)

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Trying to confirm the framework with the interviewer** ("Does that make sense?") repeatedly — reads as needing approval.

findings.section: 1.3 What weak candidates do in the first 30 seconds (red flags)
findings.text: **Showing visible panic / writing then crossing out** — readable through silence; tank impression.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"How does the company actually make money?"** — business-model question; #1 expected. [Hacking the Case Interview — Clarifying](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/clarifying-questions-to-ask-in-a-case-interview)

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"What does success look like — revenue, profit, share?"** — objective metric; rewarded because it focuses everything downstream.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Over what time horizon?"** — short vs long-term lens changes the entire framework.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Are there any constraints — budget, regulatory, M&A on/off the table?"** — surfaces unstated limits.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"What products or services do they sell?"** — basic product clarification.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Who are their customers — B2B, B2C, both?"** — channel/customer-base clarifier.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Direct to consumer or through retailers/partners?"** — distribution structure.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"One geography or multiple?"** — geography scope clarifier; rewarded because it's MECE-relevant.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"What does 'widget' [or any jargon] mean here?"** — Cosentino: never lose points for asking about jargon. [Cosentino, *Case in Point*](https://caseinterview.com/case-in-point)

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Is the decline upstream, midstream, or downstream?"** — segmentation question for revenue cases.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Are we entering organically or via acquisition?"** — market-entry clarifier; rewarded because it changes the framework branches.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Public or private company?"** — affects financing/optionality.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Who is asking — the CEO, the COO, a PE owner?"** — decision-maker identification.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Is this an industry-wide problem or company-specific?"** — narrows to internal vs external root causes.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"How long has this been happening?"** — temporal scope; rewarded because it informs hypothesis.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Just to confirm — the goal is X, and we're focused on Y. Right?"** — combined recap-as-clarification.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"What's the ask in one sentence?"** — sharp candidate move.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Is there a target number we're trying to hit?"** — targets quantitative success criterion.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Why now — is there a trigger event?"** — surface pressure source.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"What has the client already tried?"** — prevents you proposing things they've discarded.

findings.section: 2.1 Questions interviewers EXPECT (and reward)
findings.text: **"Is the company profitable today?"** — context-anchor for any growth/strategy case.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"How big is the market?"** — analysis question disguised as clarification; should appear inside structure.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"How much have revenues declined?"** — explicitly named bad up-front question.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"Who are the competitors?"** — body-of-case question, not clarification.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"What are current growth trends?"** — same — analysis, not clarification.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"How is customer satisfaction?"** — same.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"What's their cost structure?"** — analysis bucket, not clarification.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"What framework should I use?"** — biggest single tell of an unprepared candidate.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"Can you repeat the prompt?" (after 2 min)** — interviewers note inattention.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"Is there anything else I should know?"** — open-ended fishing question; usually answered "no" curtly.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **Asking 4+ clarifications back-to-back.** Reads as stalling; interviewers cap engagement.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"What does 'strategy' mean here?"** — too abstract; interviewers won't define their terms.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"Can you tell me more about the industry?"** — too broad; should be inside framework.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"What's the answer you're looking for?"** — fatal — interpreted as fishing.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **Multiple yes/no questions with no underlying logic** — feels like checklist mode.

findings.section: 2.2 Clarifying questions interviewers PUSHBACK on
findings.text: **"Should I use a framework?"** — never ask; just deliver one.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"Let's set that aside for now."** — neutral park signal.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"Let's come back to that later."** — soft redirect, common at Bain.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"Let's not go there yet."** — direct redirect when candidate jumps ahead.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"For now, focus on [X]."** — hard narrowing.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"I want to push you on something else."** — McKinsey-style topic switch.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"Let's move on."** — explicit pace signal; means "you've spent enough here."

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"Why don't we focus on [X] for now?"** — softer Bain variant.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"Park that for now and let's come back."** — explicit park language.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"Good question, but for now assume [X]."** — common deflection where interviewer hands you an assumption.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"Okay, great answer — now I'd like to switch gears."** — interviewer-led abrupt-switch standard at McKinsey.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"Let's say [X] is true for now."** — assumption-handoff to keep candidate moving.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"For the purpose of this case, assume Y."** — hard assumption injection.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"That's a different question — let's tackle it later."** — explicit topical postponement.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"What would you recommend based on what you have so far?"** — pace-compression signal that time is short.

findings.section: 2.3 Exact phrases interviewers use to redirect
findings.text: **"Oh, that's so very interesting…"** — flagged transitional language; interviewer is buying time, not endorsing.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **MECE = mutually exclusive AND collectively exhaustive.** Buckets don't overlap; together they cover the whole problem. [Hacking the Case Interview — MECE](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mece)

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **Profitability tree as MECE example:** Revenue − Cost. Then Revenue = Price × Volume; Cost = Fixed + Variable. Each split is clean. [IGotAnOffer — MECE](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/mece)

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **NOT MECE: "Price, Volume, Revenue"** — Revenue overlaps with Price × Volume. Classic interviewer trap. [NextEpMBB — MECE](https://nextepmbb.com/en/mece-framework-case-interviews-en/)

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **NOT MECE customer segmentation: "Children, Teenagers, Adults, People under 30"** — overlap in last category.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **NOT MECE: "Marketing" branch and "Revenue Growth" branch** — marketing initiatives sit in both; double-count risk.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **NOT MECE: "Millennials" and "Online shoppers"** — many millennials shop online; overlap.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **Restaurant revenue case anti-pattern:** Marketing & Brand Perception in same framework — Customer Experience feeds Brand Perception so they overlap; classic interviewer probe: "Which bucket does an ad campaign communicating bad customer experience fit into?" [NextEpMBB — MECE](https://nextepmbb.com/en/mece-framework-case-interviews-en/)

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **MECE applies at every layer of the issue tree, not just the top.** [MConsultingPrep — MECE 6 rules](https://mconsultingprep.com/case-interview-mece)

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **Stress-test MECE by asking "where does X fit?"** for any plausible factor.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **Standard MECE splits MBB candidates use:** internal/external; supply/demand; quantitative/qualitative; financial/strategic; revenue/cost; short-term/long-term.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **It's easier to check overlaps than gaps** — quick-scan buckets for bleed before presenting.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **3-4 top-level buckets is the sweet spot.** Fewer = shallow, more = unmemorable.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **Buckets must be tied to the case-specific objective**, not generic.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **The "what would have to be true?" test:** ask yourself "what 3-4 things must be true for me to confidently recommend?" — those become the buckets.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **Each bucket should be hypothesis-loaded** — name the question it answers, not just the topic.

findings.section: 3.1 What "MECE" looks like at MBB (with examples)
findings.text: **Turn paper toward interviewer when presenting** — physical handoff is part of the MECE delivery.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"How are these mutually exclusive?"** — direct MECE challenge.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"I see some overlap there."** — softer MECE flag.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Walk me through how those buckets are different."** — pushes candidate to defend MECE.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Where would [X] fit in your structure?"** — classic stress-test; restaurant ad-campaign example.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Does anything not fit into these buckets?"** — exhaustiveness probe.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Why these three?"** — forces candidate to articulate MECE rationale.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"That feels generic."** — direct call-out of a memorised framework.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"That sounds rehearsed."** — verbatim feedback documented from McKinsey interviewers. [PrepLounge — Rehearsed feedback](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/i-have-recently-received-my-feedback-on-a-case-interview-the-interviewer-mentioned-that-my-performance-in-the-case-was-great-however-it-felt-rehearsed-and-lacks-creativity-what-does-it-mean-that-it-fe-21841)

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Tell me why that's relevant here."** — challenges generic 4Ps/5Cs application.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"That's a 4-P framework — what does it actually tell us about this client?"** — direct anti-canned-framework hit.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"What's missing?"** — exhaustiveness probe.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Aren't [X] and [Y] really the same thing?"** — overlap probe.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"You haven't covered [X]."** — gap call-out.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Which of these matters most for the client?"** — prioritisation push when buckets are flat.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Where would you start?"** — same as above; forces hypothesis.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Why this order?"** — challenges candidate's prioritisation logic.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"What's your hypothesis?"** — most common follow-up to a flat structure.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Is that the most important driver?"** — challenge to first-bucket selection.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Can you go one level deeper?"** — depth push.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Break that bucket down for me."** — forces sub-trees.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"What does that bucket actually mean?"** — challenges vague label.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"That sounds like the textbook answer."** — overt anti-rote signal.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"How does that connect to [client objective]?"** — forces objective alignment.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"You're describing a generic profitability tree — make it specific to this client."** — explicit anti-rote at MBB.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"Are these collectively exhaustive?"** — direct CE probe.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"What if [factor] doesn't fall in any of these?"** — exhaustiveness probe.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"You've given me categories — what's the analysis you'd do under each?"** — pushes from labels to actions.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"That's not really a structure, that's a list."** — sharp flag for non-tree thinking.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"You've covered the same idea twice."** — overlap call-out.

findings.section: 3.2 Phrases interviewers use when structure is weak
findings.text: **"How is this different from a generic 5-Cs?"** — anti-rote probe.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"Great. Let's dig into [bucket X]."** — quick acceptance, immediate dive.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"Good — where would you start?"** — accepts structure, demands prioritisation.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"That's helpful."** — neutral-positive, conversational at Bain.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **Pace acceleration** — interviewer hands data without asking — implicit "your structure is fine, here's the data."

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"Why don't we start there."** — approval-via-action.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"That's a clear structure."** — explicit (rare) approval.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"Once you have ensured the interviewer's buy-in to your structure, you are more than halfway through the case."** — verbatim from MyConsultingCoach on what acceptance signals. [MyConsultingCoach](https://www.myconsultingcoach.com/case-interview)

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"Sounds good — what's your hypothesis?"** — accepts structure, immediately tests next layer.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **Lack of pushback / minimal probing** — itself a signal of acceptance; interviewers conserve probes for weak structures.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"OK." (short, then handing over data)** — micro-acceptance; interviewer-led shorthand.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"Let's go with that."** — clean approval.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"Take me through bucket one."** — implicit acceptance; immediately consume the structure.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"Why that one first?"** — accepts structure, tests prioritisation logic (not a knock).

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **"I like the logic."** — explicit (rare) approval; usually paired with a probe.

findings.section: 3.3 Phrases interviewers use when structure is strong
findings.text: **Interviewer starts handing exhibits** without challenging buckets — strongest non-verbal acceptance.

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **"What would you recommend based on what you have so far?"** — explicit pace-compression. Means: synthesise now.

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **"Let's move on."** — pace signal; means stop here.

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **Skipping branches** of your framework explicitly: "Let's skip [X] and go to [Y]."

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **Mini-case format**: 5-15 min instead of 30-45, compressing structure + math + recommendation. [Hacking the Case Interview — Mini case](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mini-case-interview)

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **Abrupt switch:** "Okay, great answer — now I want to ask you about something different." Standard McKinsey interviewer-led move.

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **Withholding data unless asked** — forces candidate to prioritise.

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **Setting an explicit timer:** "We have 2 minutes for this part." — common in BCG written/PEI hybrid rounds.

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **Pace-test by asking for a recommendation early:** "If I had to walk into the boardroom in 30 seconds, what would you tell me?"

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **Forcing assumption injection:** "Assume X is true and move on." — strips candidate of analysis time.

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **"Interviewers consistently say they would rather see a candidate make a quick call with limited data than watch them spin their wheels."** — verbatim from compressed-time guidance. [Hacking the Case Interview — Mini case](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mini-case-interview)

findings.section: 3.4 Time pressure tactics interviewers use
findings.text: **Hard cut-off mid-thought:** "I'm going to stop you there." — pace signal that you've drifted.

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Interviewers typically hand off numbers verbally first, then sometimes follow up with a printed exhibit — McKinsey is the most consistent in giving exhibits with explicit titles, axis labels, and legends. [Case Interview Math (IGotAnOffer)](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: The standard format is "Here are some numbers / data / an exhibit. What can you tell me from this?" — open-ended interpretation expected before any calculation. [Hacking the Case Interview — Math Guide](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: McKinsey-style PEI/case math problems are usually set up as a "given X and Y, what is Z?" format with specific values stated, expecting precise answer. [IGotAnOffer Math Guide](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Bain and BCG more often give partial data and expect the candidate to ask for what's missing rather than dumping everything upfront. [StrategyCase — Case Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Common opener phrasing: "Let me give you some numbers" / "I have some data for you" / "Take a look at this exhibit" — interviewer pauses, waits for candidate to drive. [RocketBlocks — Case Math](https://www.rocketblocks.me/blog/case-interview-math.php)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Many interviewers will deliberately read numbers out loud once and not repeat — testing note-taking discipline. [PrepLounge — How fast math](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Format expectation: candidate writes numbers neatly in columns, labels units (USD, units, %), and confirms back: "So we have revenue of $500M and costs of $400M, correct?" [CaseCoach Math Guide](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Interviewers expect candidates to take 15-30 seconds of silence to digest an exhibit before talking — silence is allowed and rewarded. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: McKinsey often introduces data with a specific question ("What's the breakeven volume?") rather than open-ended ("What do you make of this?"). [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB Guide](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Bain interviewers more often hand the exhibit and ask "What stands out to you?" — testing pattern recognition before quant. [Hacking the Case Interview Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: BCG style: chart-heavy exhibits, often with two axes and a legend; candidate must orient themselves before computing. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Standard expected handoff sequence: interviewer reads data → candidate confirms numbers → candidate states approach → candidate asks "May I take a moment?" before calculating. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Interviewers expect candidates to ALWAYS ask "Is it OK if I round?" before starting — failing to ask is a common dock. [Hacking the Case Interview Mental Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-mental-math)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Numbers given are often "messy on purpose" (e.g., $387M instead of $400M) to test whether candidate will round appropriately. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Interviewers will sometimes withhold a key number deliberately so the candidate must request it: "What additional data would you need to answer this?" [Career in Consulting Math Practice](https://careerinconsulting.com/consulting-case-interview-math-practice-guide-2024/)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: The four-step communication framework interviewers expect: "Verbalize, Calculate, Sense-check, Interpret." [CaseCoach Math](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.1 How interviewers introduce data
findings.text: Interviewers often introduce an exhibit by saying "I'll let you take a look" — candidate is expected to study it silently for 20-30 sec then narrate. [RocketBlocks Math](https://www.rocketblocks.me/blog/case-interview-math.php)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Walk me through that calculation." — most common probe when interviewer suspects an error. [Hacking the Case Interview Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Hmm, that doesn't feel right to me." — standard Bain-style soft challenge. [PrepLounge — Bain math](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Are you sure about that?" — direct McKinsey-style challenge designed to test if candidate will fold or defend. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Can you show me how you got there?" [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Talk me through your math." [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "How did you arrive at that number?" [Hacking the Case Interview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Does that answer make sense to you?" — sanity-check probe. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Is that the order of magnitude you'd expect?" [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Let me stop you there — can you double-check that?" [PrepLounge math threads](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "I'm getting a different number — can you redo that?" [RocketBlocks](https://www.rocketblocks.me/blog/case-interview-math.php)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Did you account for [X variable]?" — leading question hinting at omission. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "What unit is that in?" — when candidate forgets to label. [Hacking the Case Interview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "How many zeros are in that?" — classic Bain probe when zeros get dropped. [PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Can you write that out for me?" — forcing the candidate to slow down and externalize. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "What's your sanity check on that?" [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Just to make sure I follow — how did you get from $40M to $4M?" [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Take a step back — does that number pass the smell test?" [StrategyCase](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Are you confident in that number?" — silence after = candidate should hold or fix. [Hacking the Case Interview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Try it again." — terse McKinsey-style; no hint, just redo. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Let's pause — what's your equation here?" [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Read me back the numbers." — when candidate seems lost. [PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "What's that as a percentage of revenue?" — pushing for ratio sense. [Hacking the Case Interview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "I think you may have skipped a step." [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Can you simplify that approach?" — when candidate over-complicates. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "Why don't you start that one over?" [RocketBlocks Math](https://www.rocketblocks.me/blog/case-interview-math.php)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "What does that number actually mean for the client?" — when candidate computes but doesn't interpret. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "So what?" — classic terse follow-up after a number is delivered without context. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.2 Phrases when candidate's math drifts
findings.text: "If we round, where do we land roughly?" — push toward order-of-magnitude. [Hacking the Case Interview Mental Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-mental-math)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: McKinsey interviewers tend to push back politely but firmly: "Are you sure?" with neutral facial expression — candidate must self-check without help. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: Bain interviewers more likely to soft-correct with a hint: "Did you account for fixed costs as well?" [PrepLounge Bain math](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: BCG style is closer to McKinsey: silent challenge, candidate is expected to catch their own error. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: Tier-1 norm: a single math error is recoverable if the candidate notices and corrects without prompting. [Hacking the Case Interview MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: If the candidate insists on a wrong answer when challenged, that is a near-fatal signal — interviewers value humility over false confidence. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: If candidate folds immediately when challenged on a CORRECT answer, that's also a fail signal — interviewers sometimes challenge correct answers to test conviction. [PrepLounge math threads](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: Standard recovery is "Let me re-check that — give me a moment" → recompute aloud → "OK, I had a transcription error, the correct number is X." [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: Interviewers will often let a wrong number stand for a beat to see if the candidate sense-checks it themselves before delivering. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: At Bain, "candidates who failed math questions usually did not struggle with the actual arithmetic but failed because they jumped into calculations without a plan, lost track of zeros, or got the right number but could not explain what it meant." [Hacking the Case Interview Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: McKinsey interviewers will sometimes follow a wrong answer with another, harder question rather than correcting — the candidate's downstream logic gets evaluated against their own (wrong) number. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: If a candidate gets the wrong number but interprets it correctly, partial credit is given at all three firms. [CaseCoach Math](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: "If you realize partway through that you've made an error, stop, acknowledge, and restart that step — don't try to hide it." [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: Tier-2 firms (Deloitte S&O, Accenture Strategy) tend to coach more during math; MBB will coach less and observe more. [Hacking the Case Interview MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: After a wrong answer is corrected, interviewers usually move on without dwelling — dwelling signals the error was disqualifying. [PrepLounge math threads](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: Repeated math errors (3+) are typically a hard fail at MBB even if framework and communication are strong. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.3 How they handle a wrong answer
findings.text: McKinsey PST and digital screens grade math purely on correctness — no partial credit for "good thinking." Round-by-round MBB interviews allow more nuance. [Hacking the Case Interview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: Most case calculations should take 30-90 seconds; >2 minutes signals over-complication. [Hacking the Case Interview Mental Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-mental-math)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: McKinsey expects more precision; BCG and Bain more often allow rounding, especially in candidate-led cases. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: "Always practice without rounding — if you train for precision you can choose to round in interview." [Hacking the Case Interview Mental Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-mental-math)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: Round 1 (associate-conducted) typically more lenient on speed; Round 2/Final (partner-conducted) faster pace and less patience. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: Acceptable rounding tolerance: typically within 5-10% of the precise answer if rounding was declared upfront. [CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: ~90% of case math is multiplication, division, percentages, fractions, and basic algebra — no calculus or stats. [Hacking the Case Interview Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: Candidates should be able to multiply 2-digit by 2-digit numbers (e.g., 47 × 32) within ~15 seconds in their head. [RocketBlocks Math](https://www.rocketblocks.me/blog/case-interview-math.php)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: Expected fluency: % conversions (12.5% = 1/8, 6.67% = 1/15) on demand — pre-memorized fraction-to-percent table. [Hacking the Case Interview Mental Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-mental-math)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: Big number arithmetic (millions × millions, etc.) — candidates expected to use scientific notation or "drop and remember zeros" approach. [StrategyCase](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: McKinsey Solve / Imbellus / digital screen has no partner forgiveness — speed AND accuracy both required, no rounding. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: Bain expects "feel for numbers" over raw speed — i.e., a candidate who is 5% slow but always gets directionally right beats a fast but error-prone one. [PrepLounge how-fast-math](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: Final round at all three firms expects partner-level "back-of-envelope" math — i.e., the candidate confidently states "roughly $200M" without grinding through digits. [Hacking the Case Interview MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: For market sizing specifically: rounding is always acceptable; precision is suspicious (suggests fake data). [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: BCG's Casey/Online Test grades on speed under timed pressure — expectation tighter than live interviews. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.4 Mental math expectations by firm/round
findings.text: "If your computation goes more than ~60 seconds without you saying anything out loud, you're failing the communication bar." [CaseCoach Math](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: Order-of-magnitude probes: "Roughly how big is this market?" — interviewer wants the candidate to anchor on a known reference (e.g., US population ~330M) and reason. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: Sanity-check expectation: after every calculation, candidate should explicitly say "Does that make sense? If revenue is $500M and that's 20% of the market, market is $2.5B — checks out vs. what we know." [Hacking the Case Interview Mental Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-mental-math)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: Interviewers often deliberately let an obviously wrong number sit (e.g., a profit margin of 200%) to see if the candidate flags it. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: "Does this number pass the smell test?" — verbatim Bain probe. [PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: Reasonableness anchors interviewers expect candidates to know: US GDP ~$25T, US population ~330M, avg US household income ~$75K, avg car price ~$45K. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: "If your answer is off by 10x, you should feel it before the interviewer points it out." [Hacking the Case Interview Math](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-case-interview-math)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: Top sign of strong "number feel": candidate says "That seems high — let me re-check" BEFORE delivering, not after challenge. [CaseCoach Math](https://casecoach.com/b/case-interview-math/)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: Common test: interviewer states "We earn $50M on $40M of revenue" — candidate should immediately flag impossible margin (>100%). [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: "Feel for numbers" is graded as much as accuracy — a fast-but-uncalibrated candidate fails. [IGotAnOffer Math](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-maths)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: Bain interviewers value interpretation over computation — "What does this number mean for the client?" weighted as heavily as "What's the answer?" [PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-fast-do-i-need-to-do-math-in-a-case-interview-4516)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: McKinsey interviewers often give a number and ask "Is that big or small?" — testing whether candidate can contextualize without a benchmark handed over. [Hacking the Case Interview MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 4.5 How interviewers test "feel for numbers"
findings.text: Pattern recognition test: interviewer shows a chart with an outlier and waits silently — candidate is expected to flag the outlier unprompted. [StrategyCase Math](https://strategycase.com/case-interview-math-the-ultimate-guide/)

findings.section: 5.1 How interviewers test "drive from hypothesis"
findings.text: Case interviews are "hypothesis-driven, not framework-driven, and the hypothesis determines the choice of framework." [Victor Cheng — Case Interview Secrets summary](https://youexec.com/book-summaries/case-interview-secrets)

findings.section: 5.1 How interviewers test "drive from hypothesis"
findings.text: Victor Cheng's four consulting tools: "1. Hypothesis 2. Issue Tree/Framework 3. Drill-Down Analysis 4. Synthesis" — interviewers expect the candidate to demonstrate all four. [Shortform — Victor Cheng's framework](https://www.shortform.com/books/blog/problem-solving-framework.html)

findings.section: 5.1 How interviewers test "drive from hypothesis"
findings.text: "Drill Down Analysis" is the systematic method: at each branch, candidate analyzes data to prove or disprove that branch; if proven false, candidate revises the hypothesis and creates a new issue tree. [Hacking the Case Interview — Hypothesis](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-hypothesis)

findings.section: 5.1 How interviewers test "drive from hypothesis"
findings.text: Interviewers expect candidates to surface a hypothesis WITHIN ~60 seconds of seeing data — silence past that = they will probe. [Caseinterview.com — Importance of hypothesis](https://caseinterview.com/hypothesis-in-a-case-interview)

findings.section: 5.1 How interviewers test "drive from hypothesis"
findings.text: The "drive" signal: candidate uses each new piece of data to either confirm or revise the hypothesis — not just collect data. [MyConsultingCoach — hypothesis-driven](https://www.myconsultingcoach.com/case-interview-hypothesis-driven-structure)

findings.section: 5.1 How interviewers test "drive from hypothesis"
findings.text: Interviewers grade whether candidate explicitly says "Based on what we've seen, my updated hypothesis is..." after each data drop. [Career in Consulting — hypothesis-driven](https://careerinconsulting.com/hypothesis/)

findings.section: 5.1 How interviewers test "drive from hypothesis"
findings.text: McKinsey weighs hypothesis-driven thinking heavily because consulting work is hypothesis-driven by client mandate; framework-driven candidates fail. [Caseinterview.com — hypothesis](https://caseinterview.com/hypothesis-in-case-interview)

findings.section: 5.1 How interviewers test "drive from hypothesis"
