findings.text: Second round is often **multi-case format** at McKinsey — 2-3 back-to-back cases in one day. [Source: McKinsey Careers](https://www.mckinsey.com/careers/interviewing)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Principals deliberately **add curveballs mid-case** — e.g. new data dump, changed objective — to test adaptability. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Round two cases often draw from **the interviewer's actual project experience** — less scripted. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Principals probe **"so what?"** harder — surfacing whether the candidate can extract implications, not just analyze. [Source: Caseinterview.com synthesis](https://caseinterview.com/synthesis-in-case-interviews)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Round two tests **client-readiness** — could you walk into a client meeting tomorrow and add value? [Source: MyConsultingOffer](https://www.myconsultingoffer.org/case-study-interview-prep/final-round/)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Cases at this level often have **ambiguous objectives** — candidate must clarify and pick the right metric. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Principals frequently push back with **"why not the opposite?"** to test conviction. [Source: PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/case-interview-structure-20493)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Expect deeper **brainstorming with quantitative grounding** — "give me 5 ideas, then size each one." [Source: PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/how-can-you-brainstorm-in-a-structured-way-during-a-case-study-interview-436)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Fit questions in round two probe **leadership under conflict, ambiguity, and failure** — not just achievement stories. [Source: Medium - Antoine](https://medium.com/@zerotombb/preparing-your-personal-experience-stories-for-mckinsey-bcg-and-bain-interviews-6b11395ac4e7)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Round two interviewers **take notes more aggressively** and reference earlier comments to test consistency. [Source: PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/case-interview-structure-20493)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Principal-level interviewers often deliver the case **without notes** — they know it cold and watch you, not the page. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 10.2 Second round (Principal-level)
findings.text: Second round assesses **"would I staff this person on my team next Monday?"** — a much higher bar than round one. [Source: MyConsultingOffer](https://www.myconsultingoffer.org/case-study-interview-prep/final-round/)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partner round is run by **Partners and Senior Partners** (sometimes one per session, sometimes a panel). [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partners often **use the same case for years** — they deliver it without script, freeform. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partner cases are **less structured** — partners give candidates more room to lead. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partners spend **more time on fit/PEI** than on quant — they trust prior rounds verified case skills. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/how-different-are-the-interviews-at-mckinsey-bain-and-bcg/)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partner round has **higher stakes per slot** — they decide the offer, not just advance the candidate. [Source: Caseinterview.com](https://caseinterview.com/bcg-mckinsey-case-interviews-final-round)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partners screen for **"would I sell this person to my client?"** — executive presence, gravitas, polish. [Source: Caseinterview.com](https://caseinterview.com/bcg-mckinsey-case-interviews-final-round)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Storytelling is critical: PEI answers must show **arc, conflict, decision, and impact** — not chronological recap. [Source: Medium - Antoine](https://medium.com/@zerotombb/preparing-your-personal-experience-stories-for-mckinsey-bcg-and-bain-interviews-6b11395ac4e7)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: McKinsey's PEI specifically tests **Personal Impact, Entrepreneurial Drive, and Inclusive Leadership** at partner round. [Source: McKinsey Careers](https://www.mckinsey.com/careers/interviewing)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partners frequently interrupt to **dig into one detail for 5-10 minutes** — testing depth over breadth. [Source: Medium - Antoine](https://medium.com/@zerotombb/preparing-your-personal-experience-stories-for-mckinsey-bcg-and-bain-interviews-6b11395ac4e7)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partners probe **EQ and self-awareness** through "tell me about a failure" and follow-ups: "What did you do differently next time? What would you still change?" [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mckinsey-interview-questions)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: The partner case often becomes a **dialogue, not a quiz** — partners want to see if you can hold a peer-level conversation. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partner round may include **"why our firm?"** with very specific follow-ups about practices, recent projects, partners' clients. [Source: Sensei AI](https://www.senseicopilot.com/blog/bain-vs-bcg-vs-mckinsey-interview-differences-explained)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partners stress-test **commitment to consulting** — probing whether the candidate is using offer as backup. [Source: Caseinterview.com](https://caseinterview.com/bcg-mckinsey-case-interviews-final-round)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Bain partner round emphasizes **cultural fit ("A Bainie never lets another Bainie fail")** — collaborative storytelling. [Source: Sensei AI](https://www.senseicopilot.com/blog/bain-vs-bcg-vs-mckinsey-interview-differences-explained)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: BCG partner round emphasizes **intellectual depth and creativity** — partners often push on "what's the second-order effect?" [Source: Sensei AI](https://www.senseicopilot.com/blog/bain-vs-bcg-vs-mckinsey-interview-differences-explained)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: McKinsey partner round emphasizes **structured top-down communication** even in the fit portion. [Source: McKinsey Careers](https://www.mckinsey.com/careers/interviewing)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partners often ask **"what questions do you have for me?"** — and judge candidate by quality of questions (industry-specific > generic). [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mckinsey-interview-questions)

findings.section: 10.3 Partner round (storytelling + EQ test)
findings.text: Partners weight **the "hire / no-hire / strong yes" tier** more heavily — a soft yes from a partner often fails the candidate. [Source: Caseinterview.com](https://caseinterview.com/bcg-mckinsey-case-interviews-final-round)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: **Bar rises from "competent" to "client-ready" to "partner-track"** across rounds. [Source: MyConsultingOffer](https://www.myconsultingoffer.org/case-study-interview-prep/final-round/)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Case complexity increases: round 1 = clear prompt, round 2 = ambiguous prompt, partner = open-ended client problem. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Time pressure increases: principals expect faster synthesis; partners expect snap recommendations under data scarcity. [Source: PrepLounge](https://www.preplounge.com/en/consulting-forum/difference-between-first-round-and-second-round-interviews-9)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Fit weight grows: ~10-20% in round 1 → 30-40% in round 2 → 50%+ at partner round. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Quant rigor decreases as a screen but **errors are punished more heavily** — at partner level a single math mistake can sink the offer. [Source: Caseinterview.com](https://caseinterview.com/bcg-mckinsey-case-interviews-final-round)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Structural rigidity decreases: round 1 wants a clean MECE tree; partner wants insight-led prioritization. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-structure)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Communication standard shifts: from "clear and structured" → "executive presence" → "trusted advisor voice." [Source: MyConsultingCoach](https://www.myconsultingcoach.com/case-interview-provide-recommendations)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Curveballs increase in frequency and severity round-over-round. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: PEI depth increases: surface story → detailed actions → second/third "why?" probes. [Source: Medium - Antoine](https://medium.com/@zerotombb/preparing-your-personal-experience-stories-for-mckinsey-bcg-and-bain-interviews-6b11395ac4e7)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Mental math expectation shifts: round 1 = arithmetic accuracy; round 2 = estimation savvy; partner = number sense + "smell test." [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Hiring decision threshold tightens: a "yes" from one round-1 interviewer ≠ a "yes" from a partner. Partner-level disagreement often kills offers. [Source: Caseinterview.com](https://caseinterview.com/bcg-mckinsey-case-interviews-final-round)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Round 2/partner cases are more likely to **draw from real client engagements** the interviewer led. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Early rounds reward **process compliance** (clean steps); later rounds reward **judgment under ambiguity**. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: Interview length grows: round 1 ~30 min, round 2 ~45 min, partner often 60-90 min including coffee chat. [Source: RocketBlocks](https://www.rocketblocks.me/blog/difference-between-first-and-second-round-consulting-interviews.php)

findings.section: 10.4 What changes across rounds (bar progression)
findings.text: By partner round, the **"likeability" filter is fully active** — partners pick people they want around. [Source: Caseinterview.com](https://caseinterview.com/bcg-mckinsey-case-interviews-final-round)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't give the answer.** Interviewers are trained to redirect, not solve. If a candidate asks "is X correct?", a trained interviewer responds "what do *you* think?" [Source: Caseinterview.com](https://caseinterview.com/case_interview_frameworks.pdf)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't say "good job" mid-case.** Positive feedback distorts the candidate's path; trained interviewers stay neutral. [Source: Interview IQ poker face](https://interviewiq.com.au/how-do-you-deal-with-poker-faced-interviewers)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't laugh at wrong answers.** Trained MBB interviewers protect candidate dignity — laughing biases the rest of the interview. [Source: Camden Kelly](https://camdenkelly.com/fired-before-youre-hired-five-ways-to-ruin-any-interview/)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't course-correct prematurely.** Even if a candidate goes down a wrong branch, interviewers let them follow the logic to see if they self-correct. [Source: Caseinterview.com frameworks](https://caseinterview.com/case_interview_frameworks.pdf)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't lead with the framework hint.** Interviewers do not say "have you thought about a 4P framework?" — they wait for candidate to propose. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview McKinsey](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mckinsey-case-interview)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't react visibly to bad math.** A wince or sigh tells the candidate they're wrong before they can self-check. [Source: Interview IQ poker face](https://interviewiq.com.au/how-do-you-deal-with-poker-faced-interviewers)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't volunteer data the candidate didn't ask for.** Information is given on request — testing whether the candidate knows what to ask. [Source: PrepLounge clarifying](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/clarifying-questions-1786)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't break character to chitchat mid-case.** Disrupts the candidate's flow and biases time. [Source: Caseinterview.com frameworks](https://caseinterview.com/case_interview_frameworks.pdf)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't argue with a correct answer just to win.** Pushback is a probe; if the candidate defends well, the interviewer accepts and moves on. [Source: Road to Offer pushback](https://www.roadtooffer.com/blog/case-interview-pushback-challenge)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't reveal which answer they prefer.** A trained interviewer will probe both directions ("why not the opposite?") without telegraphing. [Source: Road to Offer pushback](https://www.roadtooffer.com/blog/case-interview-pushback-challenge)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't rescue a struggling candidate too quickly.** Letting silence sit is part of the test — composure under pause. [Source: Interview IQ poker face](https://interviewiq.com.au/how-do-you-deal-with-poker-faced-interviewers)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't ask leading questions.** "Don't you think the cost side is the issue?" violates training; better is "what would you investigate next?" [Source: Caseinterview.com frameworks](https://caseinterview.com/case_interview_frameworks.pdf)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't grade based on getting the "right" number.** Process and structure are what's scored, not the final estimate. [Source: IGotAnOffer market sizing](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/market-sizing)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't compare candidates verbally** ("the last person got this in 2 minutes"). Distorts the experience. [Source: Camden Kelly](https://camdenkelly.com/fired-before-youre-hired-five-ways-to-ruin-any-interview/)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't multitask or check phone.** Sends a "you don't matter" signal and biases recall. [Source: Camden Kelly](https://camdenkelly.com/fired-before-youre-hired-five-ways-to-ruin-any-interview/)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't cut the candidate off mid-thought** unless they're clearly off-track and time is critical. [Source: PrepLounge case structure](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/case-interview-structure-20493)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't ask trick questions with no purpose.** Every question should map to a competency on the rubric. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview behavioral](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/consulting-behavioral-fit-interview)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't share your own opinion on the case.** "I think the answer is X" biases the candidate's response. [Source: Caseinterview.com frameworks](https://caseinterview.com/case_interview_frameworks.pdf)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't drift into a coffee chat in round 1.** Round-1 must rigorously test case skill — partner-round can be more conversational. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't penalize hesitation if the candidate ends up structured.** Pause-then-organize is a positive signal, not a negative one. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview structure](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-structure)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't grade halo-effect from the resume.** Trained interviewers re-evaluate on case performance, not pre-read. [Source: Casebasix interview pitfalls](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/consulting-interview-pitfalls-avoid-rejection)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't make the candidate feel stupid.** MBB explicitly trains for psychological safety — interviewers want best performance, not embarrassed candidates. [Source: Camden Kelly](https://camdenkelly.com/fired-before-youre-hired-five-ways-to-ruin-any-interview/)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't ramble or over-explain the prompt.** Crisp, controlled delivery models executive communication. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview structure](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-structure)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't repeat the prompt unprompted.** If the candidate didn't catch it, let them ask. [Source: PrepLounge clarifying](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/clarifying-questions-1786)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't grade on personality match.** Bain/McKinsey/BCG explicitly call out unconscious bias risk in interviewer training. [Source: Casebasix interview pitfalls](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/consulting-interview-pitfalls-avoid-rejection)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't interrupt PEI stories to skip to the impact.** The story arc itself is the signal. [Source: IGotAnOffer PEI](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/mckinsey-pei-how-to-impress-your-interviewer)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't accept first answer to PEI** — drill with "tell me more about your specific role." [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview PEI](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mckinsey-pei-questions)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't use the same case across the day's panel** — risks contamination if candidates compare notes. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't assume silence = stuck.** Strong candidates pause to think; pushing them to fill silence is a tell of bad facilitation. [Source: Caseinterview.com frameworks](https://caseinterview.com/case_interview_frameworks.pdf)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't push ideology** ("the only right way is McKinsey way"). The case should be objective-neutral. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview McKinsey](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mckinsey-case-interview)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't give partial credit for a bad framework "because the candidate tried."** Standards must be consistent. [Source: Casebasix interview pitfalls](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/consulting-interview-pitfalls-avoid-rejection)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't conduct the case while reading the prompt for the first time.** Partners are expected to know the case cold. [Source: CaseCoach](https://casecoach.com/b/whats-different-between-a-first-round-and-final-round-interview-at-bain-bcg-or-mckinsey/)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't disclose the case "answer" at the end** — protects integrity of the case for future candidates. [Source: Caseinterview.com frameworks](https://caseinterview.com/case_interview_frameworks.pdf)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't use jargon the candidate likely doesn't know** without defining it. Tests communication, not insider vocabulary. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview structure](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-structure)

findings.section: 12. Anti-patterns — what interviewers DON'T do
findings.text: **Don't talk more than the candidate.** Rule of thumb: the candidate should speak ~70-80% of the time during a case. [Source: HackingTheCaseInterview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: Real Bain interviewers routinely give hints — in 120+ Bain interviews observed, one interviewer "did not do a single case without giving hints." Hints are normal, not a black mark. [PrepLounge — Needed a hint](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/needed-a-hint-in-case-interview-how-bad-is-this-8131)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: Cases are graded holistically — needing a hint once does not sink an otherwise strong case. [PrepLounge — Needed a hint](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/needed-a-hint-in-case-interview-how-bad-is-this-8131)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: Interviewers feed data when candidate has correctly asked for a specific input. Vague "tell me more about the market" gets pushback; "what's our current market share?" gets a number. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB Guide](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: Interviewers provide data when the candidate's structure is sound but execution stalls on a missing fact (not when structure itself is broken). [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: McKinsey interviewer-led format: interviewer explicitly says "Let's start by estimating the size of the U.S. beer market" and provides anchoring data. [Hacking the Case Interview — McKinsey Style](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mckinsey-style-case-interview)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: Interviewers may feed assumptions that lead to unreasonable market sizes — to test if the candidate sanity-checks the result. [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: Common feed phrase: "Shall I make my own assumptions? Or do you have any data that I should use?" — interviewer offers data after candidate proposes structure. [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: When a candidate asks for a number, interviewers provide it WITH a setup: "Good question — here's what we have…" then watch what the candidate does with it. [Crafting Cases — Examples](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: Bain interviewers frequently bridge a stuck candidate: "Let me share some data we collected from the client" — moves the case forward without grading down. [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: Interviewers rarely give the punchline — they give a fact that re-opens a closed door (e.g., "Costs are flat year-over-year" forces candidate back to revenue side). [StrategyCase — Profitability](https://strategycase.com/profitability-case-interview/)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: McKinsey-style interviewer-led cases pre-load specific data on slides/exhibits and reveal incrementally — the "give a number" is structurally baked in. [IGotAnOffer — McKinsey Case](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/115672708-mckinsey-case-interview-preparation-the-only-post-youll-need-to-read)

findings.section: 13.1 The "let me give you a number" assist — when interviewers feed data
findings.text: Recovery from a hint matters more than not needing one — "what matters more is how you repair the situation." [PrepLounge — Needed a hint](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/needed-a-hint-in-case-interview-how-bad-is-this-8131)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "Let's take a step back." — most common verbatim reset phrase from MBB interviewers. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "Let's pause for a moment — what's the question we're trying to answer here?" — forces candidate back to the prompt. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "Before you go further, where are we in the case?" — re-anchors candidate to roadmap. [Crafting Cases — Examples](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "Let's zoom out. What's your hypothesis right now?" — tests whether candidate is hypothesis-driven. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "I want to make sure we're aligned — what are you trying to figure out?" — soft reset that flags drift. [Bain Careers — Interviewing](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/interviewing/)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "Let me bring you back to the original question." — verbatim from Bain interviewers when candidate goes off-track. [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "Pause — let's recap what we've found so far." — forces synthesis mid-case. [Management Consulted — Frameworks](https://managementconsulted.com/case-interview-frameworks-mergers-acquisitions/)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "If you had to summarize where we are in 30 seconds, what would you say?" — synthesis demand. [IGotAnOffer — Examples](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-examples)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "Let's reset. Tell me your top-down structure again." — prompts candidate to redraw framework. [Crafting Cases — Examples](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "I'm going to stop you there — step back and tell me, what's the big picture?" — common McKinsey reset. [Hacking the Case Interview — McKinsey](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mckinsey-case-interview)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "Walk me back to the beginning — what was the client's goal?" — re-anchors to objective. [Bain — Preparing for the Case](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 13.2 The "let's pause and zoom out" reset — verbatim phrases
findings.text: "Forget the math for a second. Conceptually, what's going on here?" — pulls candidate out of calc rabbit-hole. [Case Interview Secrets](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "Let me rephrase the question." — direct, polite reset. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "What I'm really asking is…" — sharpens the original question. [Crafting Cases — Examples](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "Maybe I wasn't clear — the specific thing I want to know is X." — interviewer takes blame, re-clarifies. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "To put it differently…" — alternate phrasing without losing substance. [IGotAnOffer — Examples](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/case-interview-examples)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "Said another way: <restated question>." — formulaic re-anchor. [Hacking the Case Interview](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "The question I'm asking is narrower than that — specifically, X." — forces tighter scope. [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "Let me try again — focus on X, not Y." — explicit redirect. [Crafting Cases — Examples](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "Here's what I want you to do: <specific instruction>." — issues a clearer directive. [Case Interview Secrets](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "I think we got a bit lost — the question is just X." — soft acknowledgment then reset. [PrepLounge — Advice for MBB](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/advice-for-mbb-interviews-22360)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "If I were the client and asked you 'should I do X or not,' how would you answer right now?" — reframes as boardroom question. [Bain Careers — Interviewing](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/interviewing/)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "The question isn't whether — it's by how much." — sharpens decision boundary. [StrategyCase — Profitability](https://strategycase.com/profitability-case-interview/)

findings.section: 13.3 The "what I'm asking is..." rephrase — verbatim rephrasings
findings.text: "Forget the framework. Just answer this: <specific question>." — strips abstraction. [Case Interview Secrets](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: Rescue is NOT extended when candidate is non-MECE, ignores hints, and shows no structural awareness — interviewer moves on/closes case early. [PrepLounge — Room for Error](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/room-for-error-in-mbb-interviews-16957)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: A candidate who repeatedly misses the same hint signals lack of coachability — strongest "abandon" trigger. [PrepLounge — Needed a hint](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/needed-a-hint-in-case-interview-how-bad-is-this-8131)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: Interviewers help candidates who "cooperate" — i.e. acknowledge the hint, redirect cleanly, and continue. [PrepLounge — Needed a hint](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/needed-a-hint-in-case-interview-how-bad-is-this-8131)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: Interviewers stop helping when candidate panics audibly (stutters, apologizes excessively, asks for the answer). [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: Holistic grading means even a hint-heavy case can pass IF the candidate's other dimensions (synthesis, recommendation) are strong. [PrepLounge — Needed a hint](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/needed-a-hint-in-case-interview-how-bad-is-this-8131)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: Bain candidates are evaluated on whether they "build constructively on others' ideas" — stuck candidates who refuse hints get downgraded. [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: McKinsey's PEI/case combo: a weak case gets rescue if the PEI was strong; a weak PEI + stuck case = no rescue. [IGotAnOffer — McKinsey](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/115672708-mckinsey-case-interview-preparation-the-only-post-youll-need-to-read)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: Interviewers cut the case short (a "ding") when the candidate clearly cannot recover — saves time for both parties. [PrepLounge — Room for Error](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/room-for-error-in-mbb-interviews-16957)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: The single biggest "abandon" trigger: candidate doesn't realize they're stuck and keeps confidently moving in the wrong direction. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: Self-awareness + a request for a moment ("Can I take 30 seconds?") triggers rescue, not abandonment. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: Interviewers extend more help in first round than in final round — at partner-level, the bar is "no help needed." [Bain — First Round](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/bain-first-round-interview)

findings.section: 13.4 When interviewers ABANDON a stuck candidate vs help — the rescue threshold
findings.text: Repeated math errors after a correction = abandon signal; one error caught and fixed = forgivable. [PrepLounge — Room for Error](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/room-for-error-in-mbb-interviews-16957)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: Hypothesis-driven thinking from the first minute — strong candidates state a working hypothesis before exploring data. [Road to Offer — Scoring Rubric](https://www.roadtooffer.com/blog/case-interview-scoring-rubric)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: >80% of successful candidates cite hypothesis-driven thinking as the top factor in their case performance. [Hacking the Case Interview — Hypothesis](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-hypothesis)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: Top-down communication — they answer first, then justify (Pyramid Principle / "answer-first"). [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They take 60-90 seconds of structured silence before laying out a tailored framework. [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: Their structures are explicitly MECE and tailored — not memorized off-the-shelf. [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They flag uncertainty in calculations: "I'll round to make this tractable; the order of magnitude is what matters." [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They sanity-check every numerical answer: "Does $50B make sense as a market size? Let me cross-check." [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They ask 1-3 sharp clarifying questions, not 6+ vague ones. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They synthesize across exhibits, not within them — "this exhibit + the previous one tells me X." [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They call out the "so what" of every analysis without being asked. [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They adapt mid-case — drop a failing branch, pivot, and explain why. [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They confirm objective AND time horizon AND success metric upfront ("revenue or profit?", "3-year or 10-year?"). [Casebasix — Market Entry](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/market-entry-case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They engage the interviewer as a partner — "Does that approach work for you?" — not as an examiner. [Bain Careers — Interviewing](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/interviewing/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They show executive presence: steady pace, clear voice, no filler-words during synthesis. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: Their final recommendation begins with the answer ("Yes, enter — driven by X, Y, Z"), not the analysis. [Casebasix — Market Entry](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/market-entry-case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They end with risks and next steps unprompted. [DealRoom — M&A Interview](https://dealroom.net/blog/how-to-pass-an-mergers-and-acquisitions-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: Their math is shown out loud in clean steps: "Step 1: 100M households × 30% adoption = 30M users. Step 2: ..." [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They round aggressively for tractability ($1B not $987M) and flag the rounding. [Crafting Cases — Market Sizing](https://www.craftingcases.com/market-sizing-examples/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They proactively narrow scope: "There are five drivers; given time, let me focus on the top two." [Crafting Cases — Examples](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They translate analysis into client-language: "for the CEO, this means X." [StrategyCase — Profitability](https://strategycase.com/profitability-case-interview/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They incorporate hints gracefully: "That's a great point — let me adjust my structure to account for that." [PrepLounge — Needed a hint](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/needed-a-hint-in-case-interview-how-bad-is-this-8131)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They show business judgment ("90% gross margin is unusual — this might be a software business"). [StrategyCase — Profitability](https://strategycase.com/profitability-case-interview/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They differentiate fixed vs variable costs without being asked. [StrategyCase — Profitability](https://strategycase.com/profitability-case-interview/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They ask "what's the time horizon?" before recommending strategic moves. [Casebasix — M&A](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/merger-acquisition-case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They know when to stop calculating — give the answer at order-of-magnitude precision. [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They use "drivers" language: "the three drivers of profitability here are…" [StrategyCase — Profitability](https://strategycase.com/profitability-case-interview/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They link sub-conclusions to the original question — "this means we should…" [Casebasix — Pricing](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/pricing-case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They stay calm under stress-tests / curveballs — "OK, let me think about that." (No panic.) [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They quantify intangibles: "brand erosion could cost ~5% of CLV." [IGotAnOffer — Pricing](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/pricing-case-framework)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They proactively address counterfactuals: "if X were untrue, this would change because…" [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They use precise language: "gross margin" vs "net margin," "TAM" vs "SAM." [Casebasix — Pricing](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/pricing-case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They make the recommendation actionable — specific dollar amount, specific market, specific timeline. [Casebasix — Market Entry](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/market-entry-case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They distinguish between symptoms (revenue down) and causes (price war, churn spike). [StrategyCase — Profitability](https://strategycase.com/profitability-case-interview/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They bring in 2nd-order effects: "if competitors retaliate on price, our margin gain disappears." [IGotAnOffer — Pricing](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/pricing-case-framework)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They use the phrase "let me sanity-check" — explicit metacognition. [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: Their structure has 3-4 buckets, not 7+ — disciplined prioritization. [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They explicitly state "I want to test the hypothesis that X" before each branch. [Hacking the Case Interview — Hypothesis](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-hypothesis)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They use the case's own data, not generic industry knowledge, when grounding analysis. [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They handle math without a calculator — confidence + accuracy. [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They demonstrate intellectual curiosity — push back lightly on assumptions. [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They explicitly transition between phases: "OK, with that math done, let me synthesize." [Crafting Cases — Examples](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: Strong candidates cross-exhibit synthesize at BCG — building a running hypothesis, not answering each chart in isolation. [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They keep an explicit "issue tree" or roadmap visible to themselves and the interviewer. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They handle ambiguity calmly — "I'll make an assumption; please correct me if it's wrong." [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They're consistent and accurate over fast — Cheng explicitly says consistency outperforms speed. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They show "real-time flexibility" — pivot mid-case based on new data. [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They use the word "because" liberally — every claim is causally grounded. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They differentiate "what" (data) from "so what" (insight) from "now what" (action). [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They ask permission to think: "Can I take a moment to organize my thoughts?" — projects control. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They give the recommendation in a 30-second elevator pitch — answer, 3 reasons, risks, next steps. [DealRoom — M&A Interview](https://dealroom.net/blog/how-to-pass-an-mergers-and-acquisitions-interview)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They flag tradeoffs explicitly: "we get X but lose Y; on balance, X wins because…" [IGotAnOffer — Pricing](https://igotanoffer.com/blogs/mckinsey-case-interview-blog/pricing-case-framework)

findings.section: 14. Calibration Cues — Signals of a STRONG Candidate
findings.text: They check that the interviewer is following: "does that make sense so far?" — without sounding insecure. [Bain Careers — Interviewing](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/interviewing/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Jumping into math before establishing a structure — top red flag. [Casebasix — Approaching Wrong Way](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/approaching-case-interview-wrong-way)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Using a memorized framework that doesn't fit the case (Porter's 5 Forces on a profitability decline). [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Failing to clarify the objective before launching into structure. [Casebasix — Approaching Wrong Way](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/approaching-case-interview-wrong-way)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Rambling — going off-topic for 60+ seconds without a clear point. [Toggl — Interview Red Flags](https://toggl.com/blog/interview-red-flags)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Asking 6+ vague clarifying questions — signals stalling. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: No hypothesis stated — purely "data-fishing." [Hacking the Case Interview — Hypothesis](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/case-interview-hypothesis)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Treating each exhibit in isolation, never building cross-exhibit synthesis (BCG's #1 fail mode). [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Stating analysis without "so what" — listing observations, not insights. [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Recommending without quantifying — "we should enter the market" with no numbers. [Casebasix — Market Entry](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/market-entry-case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Not flagging risks — recommendation is one-sided. [DealRoom — M&A Interview](https://dealroom.net/blog/how-to-pass-an-mergers-and-acquisitions-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Math errors — and then NOT noticing the error when results are unreasonable. [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Confidently moving in the wrong direction without realizing it (top "abandon" trigger). [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Repeatedly missing the same hint — coachability red flag. [PrepLounge — Needed a hint](https://www.preplounge.com/consulting-forum/needed-a-hint-in-case-interview-how-bad-is-this-8131)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Apologizing excessively — "sorry, I'm not sure, sorry…" [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Stuttering and audible panic when given a curveball. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Asking for the answer outright. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Bottom-up communication — listing data, then trying to assemble a conclusion at the end. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Framework with 7+ buckets — undisciplined, signals memorization not thinking. [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Non-MECE structure — overlapping or incomplete buckets. [Bain Careers — Case Interview](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/case-interview/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Failure to update hypothesis after data contradicts it — sticking to a failed branch. [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Treating quantitative correctness as sufficient — ignoring strategic context. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Slow, hesitant pacing without verbalizing thinking ("dead air"). [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Pretending to know an industry term they don't — gets caught when interviewer probes. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Forgetting key numbers from earlier in the case — bad note-taking. [PrepLounge — MBB Notes](https://www.quora.com/In-the-case-interview-with-MBB-McKinsey-BCG-Bain-when-listening-to-the-case-do-you-take-notes-or-just-picture-the-situation-in-your-head-and-how-Can-you-remember-all-the-information-that-the-interviewer-gave-to-you)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Recommending without addressing the original prompt — analytical drift. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Listing pros AND cons but not making a call — fence-sitting. [Casebasix — Market Entry](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/market-entry-case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: No prioritization — "let's look at all 8 dimensions equally." [Crafting Cases — Examples](https://www.craftingcases.com/case-interview-examples/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Showing math but not labeling units (mixing $M and $B without flagging). [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Forgetting to round — chasing false precision ($987.43M instead of "~$1B"). [Crafting Cases — Market Sizing](https://www.craftingcases.com/market-sizing-examples/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Failing to sanity-check a market size that's obviously wrong (e.g., "U.S. car market is $50T"). [PrepLounge — Market Sizing](https://www.preplounge.com/en/case-interview-basics/market-sizing)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Conflating correlation with causation in synthesis. [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Verbal tics: "um," "like," "I think maybe" — destroys executive presence. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Body language tells: looking at the ceiling repeatedly, fidgeting. [4 Corner Resources — Red Flags](https://www.4cornerresources.com/blog/top-interview-red-flags/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Re-asking a question already answered — signals candidate wasn't listening. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Defensive when challenged — argues with the interviewer instead of updating. [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Recommending "more analysis needed" as the answer — non-committal. [DealRoom — M&A Interview](https://dealroom.net/blog/how-to-pass-an-mergers-and-acquisitions-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: No timeline or specific number in recommendation — abstract. [Casebasix — Market Entry](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/market-entry-case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Asks "is that right?" after every step — over-validation seeking. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Repeats the prompt verbatim instead of synthesizing/restating in own words. [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Uses "I think" in the recommendation instead of "I recommend." [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Recommendation longer than 90 seconds — verbose, not crisp. [DealRoom — M&A Interview](https://dealroom.net/blog/how-to-pass-an-mergers-and-acquisitions-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Misses the qualitative dimensions of a quant case (e.g., brand, regulation). [Casebasix — Pricing](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/pricing-case-interview-guide)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Quantifies everything but never connects to client reality. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Bringing in irrelevant industry knowledge as a "show-off" — interviewer reads as insecurity. [Bain Careers — Interviewing](https://www.bain.com/careers/hiring-process/interviewing/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Spends >3 minutes on initial framework — paralysis. [Case Interview Secrets — Cheng](https://caseinterview.com/victor-cheng)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Ignores explicit interviewer redirection ("let's not focus on that"). [Hacking the Case Interview — MBB](https://www.hackingthecaseinterview.com/pages/mbb-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Solving symptoms not causes (e.g., "raise prices" without diagnosing why margins fell). [StrategyCase — Profitability](https://strategycase.com/profitability-case-interview/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Adopts a hostile/argumentative tone when interviewer pushes back. [Casebasix — BCG](https://www.casebasix.com/pages/bcg-case-interview)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Shows analysis but never names the bottleneck driver. [StrategyCase — Profitability](https://strategycase.com/profitability-case-interview/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
findings.text: Fails to size the prize — "save costs" with no ballpark. [StrategyCase — Profitability](https://strategycase.com/profitability-case-interview/)

findings.section: 15. Calibration Cues — Signals of a WEAK Candidate
